edges.description: Maintaining greatly enlarged motor units imposes a sustained metabolic and trophic demand on the surviving motor neurons and their distal axons.
edges.source: Enlarged Reinnervated Motor Units from Prior Polio Recovery
edges.target: Chronic Motor Neuron and Terminal Axon Metabolic Overload

edges.description: Chronic metabolic overload of overextended motor neurons leads to degeneration of the most distal terminal sprouts, beginning the denervation process.
edges.source: Chronic Motor Neuron and Terminal Axon Metabolic Overload
edges.target: Distal Axonal Sprout Degeneration and Progressive Denervation

edges.description: Ongoing denervation and motor neuron stress are accompanied by inflammatory changes in the spinal cord and elevated pro-inflammatory cytokines, which may further amplify denervation.
edges.source: Distal Axonal Sprout Degeneration and Progressive Denervation

nodes.description: Decades of sustaining abnormally enlarged motor units place chronic metabolic and bioenergetic stress on the overextended surviving motor neurons and their distal axons. This overload, together with normal age-related attrition of motor neurons, is proposed to render the terminal sprouts of these units progressively unable to be maintained, setting the stage for distal degeneration.
nodes.id: Chronic Motor Neuron and Terminal Axon Metabolic Overload

evidence.reference_title: Post-polio Syndrome: More Than Just a Lower Motor Neuron Disease.
evidence.snippet: Metabolic stress (11, 20), overuse (21, 22), physiological aging (20, 23), and persistent inflammation (24) are also thought to contribute to gradual motor unit failure.

Contributing factors to PPS may be aging (with 
motor neuron loss), overuse, and disuse.

nodes.description: Progressive degeneration of the distal terminal sprouts of the enlarged post-polio motor units produces ongoing denervation of muscle fibers. Because reinnervation by the same overburdened motor neurons can no longer fully compensate, denervation outpaces reinnervation, leading to net loss of functioning motor units. Electrophysiologically this is reflected by increased jitter, blocking, increased fiber density, and spontaneous activity in newly weakened muscles, and clinically by new weakness and atrophy.
nodes.id: Distal Axonal Sprout Degeneration and Progressive Denervation

evidence.reference_title: Disintegration of the motor unit in post-polio syndrome. Part II.
evidence.snippet:
PPMA occurring later in life represents disintegration of the 
previously reinnervated motor units.

evidence.reference_title: Disintegration of the motor unit in post-polio syndrome. Part II.
evidence.snippet:
we found in newly weakened muscles: spontaneous activity, high 
percentage of complex potentials, increased jitter, increased FD.

associated with an ongoing process of denervation and reinnervation, 
reaching a point at which denervation is no longer compensated for by 
reinnervation.

nodes.description: During and after acute paralytic poliomyelitis, poliovirus-induced anterior horn cell loss leaves muscle fibers denervated. Surviving lower motor neurons reinnervate these orphaned fibers through terminal and collateral axonal sprouting at the neuromuscular junction, producing greatly enlarged motor units that can innervate several times the normal number of muscle fibers. This compensatory reinnervation underlies the functional recovery seen after acute polio but creates motor units far larger than physiological.
nodes.id: Enlarged Reinnervated Motor Units from Prior Polio Recovery

evidence.reference_title: Post-polio Syndrome: More Than Just a Lower Motor Neuron Disease.
evidence.snippet: Following the acute phase, axonal sprouting takes place reinnervating the muscle of the affected regions

evidence.reference_title: Disintegration of the motor unit in post-polio syndrome. Part I.
evidence.snippet:
It seems that the reinnervation after acute polio is a 
continuing process and thus complete stabilization and integration of the motor 
unit cannot be achieved.

nodes.description: Inflammatory changes in the spinal cord on post-mortem examination, together with increased serum and CSF levels of pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-alpha, IFN-gamma) and inflammatory changes in skeletal muscle, suggest a low-grade inflammatory or immune component to PPS. Inflammation is proposed as a contributing factor to motor unit failure rather than the primary cause, and is not equivalent to poliovirus reactivation; its causal role remains debated.

evidence.reference_title: Post-polio Syndrome: More Than Just a Lower Motor Neuron Disease.
evidence.snippet: This hypothesis originates from post mortem observations of inflammatory changes in the spinal cord of PPS patients

The cause of this denervation is unknown, but an inflammatory 
process is possible.
